---
title: "A Signal-Sorted Long-Short Portfolio"
subtitle: "An FF5 example (synthetic data)"
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
---

## Question

Does a simple accounting signal earn a long-short return
that the five Fama-French factors do not span? Each month we
sort firms on a lagged signal, form a self-financing
top-minus-bottom-decile portfolio, and regress its return on
the five factors. All data are simulated by a seeded script;
the econometrics is illustrative scaffolding for the build
pipeline, not the subject.

## Specification

$$
\text{ls\_ret}_t = \alpha
  + \beta_1\,\text{Mkt-RF}_t + \beta_2\,\text{SMB}_t
  + \beta_3\,\text{HML}_t + \beta_4\,\text{RMW}_t
  + \beta_5\,\text{CMA}_t + \varepsilon_t
$$

The intercept $\alpha$ is the part of the long-short return
the factors do not explain.

## Result

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
# Wrap the fixed-width table in a fenced code block so the
# columns render verbatim. Without the fence, the dashed
# rule under the title is parsed as a Markdown heading and
# the alignment collapses.
cat("```text\n")
cat(readLines("output/tables/ff5_alpha.txt"), sep = "\n")
cat("\n```\n")
```

![Cumulative long-short return](output/figures/cumret.png)

The estimated monthly alpha is about 0.34 percent (t near
2.6), consistent with the value built into the simulation.
Four of the five factor loadings are statistically
indistinguishable from zero; only HML carries a marginal
load (t near -1.8, significant at the 10 percent level). The
alpha is the piece the factors do not explain.
